title: Environment Specific Vs Shared Service Model Semantic Contract
description: Coverage-complete semantic contract for the environment specific vs shared service model capability. Semantic information captured as a USF coverage record via the coverage record and equivalence audit; no external source code is imported and the V2 implementation language is an open decision.
aiGuidance: Coverage-complete draft grounded in semantic information (USF coverage record only; no source import; language open). Proof is hermetic/stale lineage, not current readiness. Enrich detail and publish fresh proof before generation; respect the hexagonal constraint in ADR 0005.

lifecycle.description: Classification model that decides whether each service is environment-specific or shared and governs the routing and isolation that follows; it is provider-backed but owns no database schema. It deploys a classification contract (V1 contract n/a) and must keep its lifecycle/readiness model unchanged during extraction, with migrations limited to contract/config references that must update semantic artefacts when behaviour changes. Rollback restores the previous classification implementation and re-runs the named proof evidence.

stateModel.description: The state is the per-service classification (environment-specific vs shared) and the routing/isolation rules derived from it, held as configuration rather than owned tenant rows. It has no owned database schema, so backup scope is configuration/evidence only and restore cannot create tenant data. Classification changes propagate to how services are resolved per environment.

permissions.description: It owns no tenant records and exposes no tenant-mutating routes, so standard platform security controls apply; its tenant impact is indirect through the isolation it enforces. The classification determines whether a service instance is shared across environments or isolated to one, which is an operator/governance concern rather than an end-user permission. Correct classification is what keeps environment boundaries from leaking.

contracts.description: The contract is the classification decision and the routing/isolation guarantees it implies for consumers, mediated through the configuration plane rather than a tenant API. Configuration is read from the declared provider/config plane and missing or invalid configuration fails closed instead of inventing defaults. Consumers rely on the classification to know whether a dependency is environment-specific or shared.

validation.description: Validation ensures every relevant service has an explicit, consistent classification and that shared-versus-specific assignments do not violate environment isolation. Drift or configuration loss is the failure to guard against. Misclassification that would cross an environment boundary is rejected.

errorModel.description: Provider failure returns a typed degraded/readiness failure, keeps mutation boundaries closed, and does not fabricate successful provider state; degraded mode disables dependent side-effect paths while keeping read-only readiness and audit visible. The risk class is semantic drift or configuration loss, not tenant data loss. Blocked readiness or a failing promotion proof is alertable.

auditModel.description: Because it owns no tenant mutations, its audit concern is the recorded classification lineage: changes to whether a service is shared or environment-specific are captured as reviewable configuration/semantic changes. Readiness and audit remain visible in degraded mode. This preserves an honest record of isolation decisions.

readinessModel.description: Readiness means every service's environment classification is present, consistent, and enforced, and the routing/isolation it implies resolves; unavailable dependencies fail closed through the readiness model. Readiness is shown by re-running the environment-service-classification document and named proof. Stale or unknown classifications do not count as ready.

proof.description: Proof is the environment-service-classification document and associated checks demonstrating consistent shared-versus-environment-specific classification; it is a foundation-document proof rather than a live runtime probe. Proof is hermetic/runtime lineage; fresh USF proof pending USF-101/USF-59.

uiSemanticDefinition.description: No distinct UI capability at the contract level (coverage matrix ui=0); UI is modelled separately and remains a future AI-customised deliverable.
